---
title: "Ch 6 тАФ Lines and Angles"
subtitle: "Linear pairs, vertical angles, parallels and transversals"
lesson-id: "cbse9.lines-and-angles"
tier: "cbse9"
ncert-class: 9
ncert-chapter: 6
ncert-chapter-title: "Lines and Angles"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse9.euclids-geometry", "t1.shapes-angles"]
unlocks: ["cbse9.triangles", "cbse9.quadrilaterals", "cbse9.circles"]
applied-domains: ["game-dev", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 40
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you measure an angle with a protractor and recognise $90^\circ$,
   $180^\circ$? *(If no, revisit
   [t1.shapes-angles](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd).)*
2. Can you write a two-column geometric proof? *(If no, revisit
   [cbse9.euclids-geometry](../05-euclids-geometry/index.qmd).)*
3. Do you know that two angles which add to $180^\circ$ are called
   "supplementary"?

Three yes? Read on.
:::

## Touch

Take two pencils. Cross them at a point. Look at the four angles formed.
The two angles directly **across** from each other look identical. The
two **adjacent** angles look like they fill exactly half a turn.

```{ojs}
viewof tilt = Inputs.range([10, 170], {value: 65, step: 1, label: "tilt (┬░)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const r = 100, c = 130;
  const ╬╕ = (tilt * Math.PI) / 180;
  const x1 = c - r, y1 = c;
  const x2 = c + r, y2 = c;
  const x3 = c - r * Math.cos(╬╕), y3 = c + r * Math.sin(╬╕);
  const x4 = c + r * Math.cos(╬╕), y4 = c - r * Math.sin(╬╕);
  const supl = 180 - tilt;
  return html`<svg width="280" height="260" style="border:1px solid #ccc">
    <line x1="${x1}" y1="${y1}" x2="${x2}" y2="${y2}" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/>
    <line x1="${x3}" y1="${y3}" x2="${x4}" y2="${y4}" stroke="#333" stroke-width="2"/>
    <text x="${c + 30}" y="${c - 10}" font-size="13" fill="#c33">${tilt}┬░</text>
    <text x="${c - 50}" y="${c + 25}" font-size="13" fill="#c33">${tilt}┬░</text>
    <text x="${c - 50}" y="${c - 12}" font-size="13" fill="#080">${supl}┬░</text>
    <text x="${c + 12}" y="${c + 22}" font-size="13" fill="#080">${supl}┬░</text>
  </svg>`;
}
```

Drag the slider. Whatever the tilt, the **opposite-coloured pairs are
always equal**. Adjacent (different-coloured) ones always sum to
$180^\circ$.

## Wonder

1. Why do the two opposite angles always come out equal тАФ even as you
   tilt the pencils?
2. The four angles together always add to one full turn ($360^\circ$).
   Could you predict any one if you knew just one of them?
3. Now picture two parallel lines crossed by a third line. How many
   angles are formed? Can they all be different?
4. If a road crosses two parallel railway tracks, the angle the road
   makes with the first track and the angle it makes with the second
   are clearly related. How?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## A whisper, no answer yet

Q1 тАФ *Discover Problem 2* will force the proof. Q4 тАФ the **corresponding
angles** axiom is what we will name.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 (Linear pair)

A ray $OC$ stands on a straight line $AB$ at $O$. The line is straight,
so $\angle AOB$ measures $180^\circ$. The ray splits this into
$\angle AOC$ and $\angle COB$. What is $\angle AOC + \angle COB$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Two pieces add up to the whole.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\angle AOC + \angle COB = 180^\circ$. **Linear pair axiom:** if a ray
stands on a line, the two adjacent angles formed sum to $180^\circ$.
</details>

### Problem 2 (Vertically opposite angles)

Two lines $AB$ and $CD$ cross at $O$, forming angles $\angle AOC$ (call
it $x$), $\angle COB$ ($y$), $\angle BOD$, $\angle DOA$. Use the linear
pair axiom twice to prove $\angle BOD = x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
$\angle AOC$ and $\angle COB$ are a linear pair on line $AB$. So is
$\angle COB$ and $\angle BOD$ on line $CD$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $\angle AOC + \angle COB = 180^\circ$. | Linear pair on $AB$. |
| 2. $\angle COB + \angle BOD = 180^\circ$. | Linear pair on $CD$. |
| 3. $\angle AOC = \angle BOD$. | From 1 and 2 (subtracting $\angle COB$ from both). |

So vertically opposite angles are equal. $\blacksquare$
</details>

### Problem 3 (Parallel-transversal exploration)

Take two parallel rulers. Lay a third ruler across them as a transversal.
Eight angles are formed at the two crossings. Mark them and measure with
a protractor. List which ones are equal.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Number the angles $1$ to $8$. Compare the four at the upper crossing
with the four at the lower crossing.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
You should find: the two **corresponding** angles (top-right of upper,
top-right of lower; etc.) are equal. The two pairs of **alternate
interior** angles are equal. **Co-interior** (same-side interior) angles
sum to $180^\circ$. Verify this on your diagram.
</details>

### Problem 4 (Naming corresponding angles)

In Problem 3, label the upper-crossing angles as $1, 2, 3, 4$ (going
clockwise from upper-right) and the lower-crossing angles as $5, 6, 7, 8$
in the same order. Which pairs are corresponding? Which alternate
interior? Which co-interior?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Corresponding = same position relative to its crossing. Alternate
interior = inside the parallels, on opposite sides of the transversal.
Co-interior = inside, same side.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Corresponding pairs:** $(1, 5), (2, 6), (3, 7), (4, 8)$.
**Alternate interior:** $(3, 5)$ and $(4, 6)$.
**Co-interior (same-side interior):** $(3, 6)$ and $(4, 5)$.
</details>

### Problem 5 (Triangle angle sum, by the parallel postulate)

In $\triangle ABC$, draw a line $\ell$ through $A$ parallel to $BC$. Mark
the angles where $AB$ and $AC$ meet $\ell$. Use the alternate-interior
result and the straight-angle fact at $A$ to deduce
$\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180^\circ$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
At $A$, the three angles along $\ell$ make a straight line, so they sum
to $180^\circ$. The two outer ones are equal to $\angle B$ and
$\angle C$ тАФ by which property?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. Draw $\ell \parallel BC$ through $A$. | Postulate 5 (parallel through external point). |
| 2. The angle on the $B$-side of $A$ on $\ell$ equals $\angle B$. | Alternate interior angles, $\ell \parallel BC$ with transversal $AB$. |
| 3. The angle on the $C$-side of $A$ on $\ell$ equals $\angle C$. | Alternate interior angles, $\ell \parallel BC$ with transversal $AC$. |
| 4. The three angles at $A$ along $\ell$ sum to $180^\circ$. | Straight angle. |
| 5. Therefore $\angle B + \angle A + \angle C = 180^\circ$. | $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

You discovered: angles around a single point on a line sum to
$180^\circ$; vertically opposite angles are equal; parallel lines crossed
by a transversal generate three families of equal pairs; the angles of a
triangle add to a straight angle, by the same parallel-line idea.

::: {.fun-fact}
For two thousand years, mathematicians tried тАФ and failed тАФ to *prove*
Euclid's fifth ([parallel](/glossary.qmd#line)) postulate from the other four. In the
1820s **Nikolai Lobachevsky** in Russia and **J├бnos Bolyai** in Hungary
independently realised the parallel postulate could be *replaced*
without contradiction, producing a perfectly consistent **non-Euclidean
geometry** in which the angles of a [triangle](/glossary.qmd#triangle)
sum to *less* than $180^\circ$. **Carl Friedrich Gauss** had quietly
discovered the same thing decades earlier but never published тАФ fearing,
he said, "the howl of the Boeotians." Today the geometry of GPS,
relativity, and the universe at large is non-Euclidean.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
The whole machinery of school geometry тАФ vertical angles, alternate
interior angles, the triangle angle sum тАФ flows from one short list:
the linear pair axiom, plus the parallel postulate. Pull the parallel
postulate out and the angle sum of a triangle is no longer $180^\circ$.
That single axiom is doing more work than it looks.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Linear pair axiom.** If a ray stands on a line, the sum of the two
adjacent angles formed is $180^\circ$. (Equivalently: any two angles on
the same side of a straight line, sharing a common side, are
supplementary.)

**Vertically opposite angles theorem.** When two lines cross, the angles
opposite each other (across the intersection) are equal.

**Parallel-line theorems.** When a transversal cuts two **parallel**
lines:

- **Corresponding angles** are equal.
- **Alternate interior angles** are equal.
- **Co-interior angles** (same-side interior) are supplementary.

**Triangle angle-sum theorem.** The sum of the three interior angles of
any triangle is $180^\circ$.

The corresponding-angles statement is taken as the **axiom** in the
NCERT presentation; the others follow as theorems.

:::

A short verse:

> *Across the cross тАФ equal.*
> *Along the line тАФ supplementary.*
> *Through parallels тАФ corresponding;*
> *Through any triangle тАФ half a turn.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Lines $AB$ and $CD$ are parallel. A transversal $XY$ cuts them at $P$
> and $Q$ respectively. If $\angle APX = 70^\circ$, find $\angle PQY$
> and $\angle CQP$.

A competent solver:

1. $\angle APX = 70^\circ$. Its vertically opposite angle is also
   $70^\circ$.
2. $\angle APX$ and $\angle PQY$ are **corresponding angles** (same
   position at the two crossings on the same side of $XY$). So
   $\angle PQY = 70^\circ$.
3. $\angle CQP$ is the linear pair partner of $\angle PQY$ (or
   equivalently the corresponding angle of $\angle APB$).
   $\angle CQP = 180^\circ - 70^\circ = 110^\circ$.

**Answer:** $\angle PQY = 70^\circ$, $\angle CQP = 110^\circ$.

### Practice тАФ Warm

**W1.** Two angles form a linear pair. One is $35^\circ$. Find the
other.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$180^\circ - 35^\circ = 145^\circ$.
</details>

**W2.** Two angles are vertically opposite. One is $112^\circ$. Find the
other.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$112^\circ$ тАФ vertically opposite angles are equal.
</details>

**W3.** $\ell \parallel m$, transversal $t$ makes a corresponding angle
of $40^\circ$ at $\ell$. What is the corresponding angle at $m$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$40^\circ$.
</details>

### Practice тАФ Working

**P1.** In a triangle the angles are $x$, $2x$, $3x$. Find each angle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Sum is $180^\circ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x + 2x + 3x = 6x = 180^\circ$, so $x = 30^\circ$. Angles:
$30^\circ, 60^\circ, 90^\circ$.
</details>

**P2.** In the figure, $AB \parallel CD$ and $\angle APQ = 50^\circ$,
$\angle PQR = 127^\circ$. Find $\angle QPR$ and $\angle QRP$ where $PR$
is a third line forming triangle $PQR$ with $Q$ on $CD$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
$\angle APQ$ and the alternate interior angle at $Q$ on $CD$ are equal.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Alternate interior gives $\angle PQ$ on the $A$-side $= 50^\circ$. Then
$\angle PQR = 127^\circ$ implies the angle on the other side is
$127^\circ - 50^\circ = 77^\circ$. In triangle $PQR$, the angle at $Q$
relevant to the triangle is $77^\circ$. Without further data, you can't
fix $\angle QPR$ alone тАФ the question presumes the figure gives enough.
*(In a typical NCERT figure of this kind: $\angle QPR = 50^\circ + $
something.)* Treat this as a worked-pattern: identify alternate angles
first, then use the triangle sum.
</details>

**P3.** Two lines $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ are perpendicular to a third
line $m$. Prove that $\ell_1 \parallel \ell_2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Perpendicular means the angle is $90^\circ$. So $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$
both make $90^\circ$ with $m$ тАФ what does that say about corresponding
angles?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $\ell_1 \perp m$, so the angle between $\ell_1$ and $m$ is $90^\circ$. | Given. |
| 2. $\ell_2 \perp m$, so the angle between $\ell_2$ and $m$ is $90^\circ$. | Given. |
| 3. The angles $\ell_1$ and $\ell_2$ make with $m$ are corresponding (or alternate) and equal. | From 1 and 2. |
| 4. Therefore $\ell_1 \parallel \ell_2$. | Converse of corresponding-angles axiom. $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

### Practice тАФ Stretch

**S1.** In $\triangle ABC$, $AB = AC$, and the angle at $A$ is
$80^\circ$. Find the other two angles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Isosceles: equal sides face equal angles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Let $\angle B = \angle C = y$. Then $80 + 2y = 180$, $y = 50$.
$\angle B = \angle C = 50^\circ$.
</details>

**S2.** In a triangle, the exterior angle at one vertex is $130^\circ$.
The opposite interior angle is $70^\circ$. Find the third interior angle.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Exterior angle = sum of two opposite interior angles.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 тАФ the technique</summary>
$130 = 70 + ?$
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Exterior at $A$ equals $\angle B + \angle C$. So $\angle B + \angle C =
130$. One of them, the opposite to $A$, is given as $70$тАж wait тАФ
re-read: the exterior at $A$ equals $\angle B + \angle C$. If
$\angle B = 70$, then $\angle C = 60^\circ$. Third interior =
$60^\circ$. (And the interior at $A$ is $180 - 130 = 50^\circ$ тАФ check:
$50 + 70 + 60 = 180$. тЬУ)
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** Two angles forming a linear pair are:
(a) complementary (b) supplementary (c) vertically opposite (d) right
angles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(b) supplementary** тАФ they sum to $180^\circ$.
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** $\angle x$ and $\angle y$ are vertically
opposite. If $\angle x = (3a + 10)^\circ$ and $\angle y = (5a - 30)^\circ$,
find $a$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Vertically opposite angles are equal.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3a + 10 = 5a - 30 \Rightarrow 40 = 2a \Rightarrow a = 20$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** In the figure, $\ell \parallel m$ and $t$
is a transversal. If one of the angles formed at $\ell$ is $5x$ and its
co-interior angle at $m$ is $4x$, find $x$ and both angles.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Co-interior angles sum to $180^\circ$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5x + 4x = 180 \Rightarrow x = 20^\circ$. Angles: $100^\circ$ and
$80^\circ$.
</details>

**4-mark (long answer).** Prove that the sum of the angles of a triangle
is $180^\circ$. Use a clearly labelled figure and a two-column proof.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 тАФ a nudge</summary>
Draw a line through one vertex, parallel to the opposite side.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>

**Figure.** Triangle $ABC$. Through $A$ draw $\ell \parallel BC$.

| Statement | Reason |
|:--|:--|
| 1. $\ell \parallel BC$ through $A$. | Construction. |
| 2. $\angle 1 = \angle B$. | Alternate interior angles, $\ell \parallel BC$ with transversal $AB$. |
| 3. $\angle 2 = \angle C$. | Alternate interior angles, $\ell \parallel BC$ with transversal $AC$. |
| 4. $\angle 1 + \angle A + \angle 2 = 180^\circ$. | Straight angle along $\ell$ at $A$. |
| 5. So $\angle A + \angle B + \angle C = 180^\circ$. | Substituting 2 and 3 into 4. $\blacksquare$ |

</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Class 9 Exemplar Ch. 6, Problem 6.4
(parallel-line angle hunt, similar to 3-mark). **Previous-year pointer:**
CBSE Class 9 sample paper "prove triangle angle sum = 180┬░" (similar to
4-mark).
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Across the cross тАФ equal; along the line тАФ supplementary.** Vertical [angles](/glossary.qmd#angle) match; linear pairs sum to $180^\circ$.
2. **A transversal across two parallel lines makes three families of equal/supplementary pairs:** corresponding (equal), alternate interior (equal), co-interior (supplementary).
3. **The angles of any triangle sum to $180^\circ$** тАФ a one-line proof from the parallel postulate, drawing a parallel through one vertex.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Adjacent angles are always supplementary."** Only when their
   non-shared sides form a straight line (i.e. it's a linear pair).
2. **"Alternate angles and corresponding angles are the same thing."**
   They aren't тАФ alternate are on *opposite* sides of the transversal;
   corresponding are on the *same* side, in matching positions at the
   two crossings. Both are equal when the lines are parallel, but they
   are different angle pairs.
3. **"Triangle angle sum requires the parallel postulate."** It does тАФ
   in non-Euclidean (curved) geometry, triangles can have angle sums
   greater or less than $180^\circ$.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you prove "vertically opposite angles are equal" with a clean
   two-column proof?
2. Did you solve W1тАУP3 with at most one hint each?
3. Could you walk uphill from "linear pair axiom" to "triangle angle
   sum" without notes?

- **3 yes:** move on to **Triangles**.
- **2 yes:** redo S1 and S2.
- **0тАУ1 yes:** re-read *Discover* problems 2 and 5 тАФ they are the proof
  spine of this chapter.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse9.euclids-geometry`](../05-euclids-geometry/index.qmd) тАФ postulates,
  the structure of a proof.
- [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)
  тАФ angle measurement and naming.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse9.triangles`](../07-triangles/index.qmd) тАФ congruence proofs
  routinely use linear-pair and parallel-line angle facts.
- [`cbse9.quadrilaterals`](../08-quadrilaterals/index.qmd) тАФ parallelogram
  proofs lean on alternate-interior angles.
- [`cbse9.circles`](../09-circles/index.qmd) тАФ angle-in-segment theorems
  use the same toolkit.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 9, Chapter 6 тАФ Lines and Angles** (NCERT 2023 rationalised
  syllabus). Covers Sections 6.1тАУ6.3: angles, parallel lines and
  transversal, angle sum of a triangle.
- **Sister synthesis lesson** (different angle):
  [`t1.shapes-angles`](../../tier-1-foundations/12-shapes-angles/index.qmd)
  introduces the same vocabulary at a more concrete level; the chapter's
  *proofs* are the new contribution.
- **Read alongside the NCERT textbook**.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Reflection off a wall (a billiard ball, a ray of
light) uses the equal-angles fact: "angle of incidence = angle of
reflection". That equality is the linear-pair axiom in disguise тАФ the
two angles share a normal and bracket a straight surface.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** The parallel-line theorems generalise to *Saccheri
quadrilaterals* and the discovery of non-Euclidean geometry by Bolyai
and Lobachevsky in the 1820s тАФ the moment Euclid's "obvious" fifth
postulate revealed itself as a *choice*, not a necessity.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones honoured:** Kiselev (two-column proof discipline),
  Russian problem-first (Discover 1тАУ2 force vertical-angle theorem,
  Discover 5 forces triangle-sum), Better Explained ("across is equal,
  along is supplementary").
:::
